I am trying to track what I spend

But I don’t know exactly what I have

Because I have accounts with multiple currencies 

Which makes me feel annoyed, confused, stressed", "aiResponse": "- Target user: Present ("I am an international student") but not very specific — consider adding context (country of study, exchange vs full-degree, part-time worker, financial literacy) and fix the article: "an international student".
- Outcome: Present ("trying to track what I spend") but vague — clarify what “track” means (e.g. see total balance, monitor monthly spending, or stay within a budget).
- Barrier/problem: There is a barrier ("I don’t know exactly what I have") but it’s imprecise — specify whether you mean current balances, net worth, purchasing power, or reconciled transactions.
- Root cause: Clear and useful ("accounts with multiple currencies") — good causal statement; you might also note related causes (exchange rates, fees, delayed feeds).
- Emotion: Included and helpful for empathy ("annoyed, confused, stressed") — keeps the statement user-centred.
- Suggested rewrite (keeps template): “I am an international student trying to see my total available funds and monthly spending, but I can’t because my money is split across accounts in multiple currencies. This makes me feel confused and stressed.”
- Tip: Make the outcome measurable (e.g. “know my total balance converted to one currency” or “keep monthly spending within £X”) and add any contextual qualifiers if you need to prioritise users.

{"title": "Write your problem statement", "userText": "I am an early-stage woman founder building a startup.

I am trying to get quick advice on business challenges like fundraising, hiring, or product decisions.

But it’s difficult to find trusted people who understand my situation and can respond quickly.

Because most networks require formal mentorship, scheduled meetings, or access to exclusive founder communities.

Which makes me feel isolated, uncertain about my decisions, and slower in moving forward with my startup.", "aiResponse": "- Target user: Yes — “an early-stage woman founder building a startup” is a clear user. If you want to narrow focus further, consider adding stage (pre-seed/seed), sector or geography so solutions don’t try to be too broad.
- Outcome: Present — “get quick advice on business challenges like fundraising, hiring, or product decisions.” To make it a stronger outcome, state the user value (e.g. “make faster, more confident decisions”) and, if useful, a time bound for “quick” (hours, same day).
- Barrier/problem: Present — “difficult to find trusted people who understand my situation and can respond quickly” is a good problem statement. You could tighten it by defining “trusted” (peers, ex-founders, investors) and “understand my situation” (stage, sector, funding status).
- Root cause: Present — you list structural causes (“most networks require formal mentorship, scheduled meetings, or access to exclusive founder communities”). That’s plausible, but check whether other root causes matter (cost, geographic/timezone mismatch, lack of lightweight matching) so you don’t lock into a particular explanation too early.
- Emotions: Present and helpful — “isolated, uncertain… slower” gives useful context about user pain and urgency.
- Focus advice: Consider choosing one primary problem to solve (speed of response, trust/relevance of advice, or accessibility/exclusivity) so your later solution can be evaluated against a clear success metric.
- Make outcomes measurable: Add one or two simple metrics you’d aim to improve (e.g. average time to get actionable advice, % increase in decision confidence, number of trusted contacts available).

{"title": "Write your problem statement", "userText": "I am the founder of TransFoodMIssion a food upcycling company.
I am trying to bring more upcycled products to the market and list them on supermarket shelves so consumers have access to them.
But it seems to be a lengthy process to involve the right stakeholders.
Because our know-how, namely the ingredients, applications, suppliers, manufacturers, and possibilities sit in a messy database, emails, and matchmaking relies solely on me.
Which makes me feel frustrated, that I am always behind and that upcycling is not reaching it's full potential.
", "aiResponse": "- You do name a specific user: “the founder of TransFoodMission” — that’s clear. Decide whether the problem is unique to founders or should read more broadly (e.g. founder/partnerships lead or operations manager) so the target user is unambiguous.  
- The desired outcome is present (“bring more upcycled products to the market and list them on supermarket shelves so consumers have access to them”), but it would be stronger if made measurable and time-bounded (e.g. number of SKUs, percentage growth, or a timeframe).  
- The barrier is stated (“a lengthy process to involve the right stakeholders”) but is a bit vague — name which stakeholders and which steps slow you down (sourcing, formulation, certification, procurement, negotiation, etc.).  
- The root cause is clear and useful: fragmented know‑how across a messy database and emails, and a single‑person matchmaking dependency. Consider tightening this to “fragmented knowledge and a single point of failure.”  
- The emotional line works — it conveys real pain — but fix the small grammar issue (“its”, not “it's”) and consider shortening for punch: “frustrated and overwhelmed that upcycling isn’t reaching its full potential.”  
- Suggested tightened problem statement (use this as a template): “I am the founder/partnerships lead at TransFoodMission trying to get X upcycled products listed in Y supermarkets within Z months so consumers can buy them, but engaging the right buyers and manufacturers takes too long because our knowledge about ingredients, suppliers and production is fragmented across spreadsheets and emails and matchmaking relies solely on me. This makes me feel frustrated and overwhelmed.”  
- Small note: keeping the outcome measurable and the barrier specific will make it much easier to test and prioritise solutions.

{"title": "Write your problem statement", "userText": "I am a senior in-house recruiter in a competitive tech market.

I am trying to build a candidate fit score agent that can identify the most viable candidates for niche roles in real-time from high volumes of CVs, LinkedIn profiles, and existing ATS talent, based on deeper fit signals like: compensation and industry alignment, business model experience, seniority depth, and ways-of-working compatibility, etc, that will double the hiring manager interview pass through rate and reduce bias by 50%.  

But screening and prioritizing candidates is slow and cognitively demanding.

Because search tools rely mostly on titles and keywords

Which makes me feel like I’m spending too much time evaluating candidates who are unlikely to convert.", "aiResponse": "- Target user: Yes — “a senior in‑house recruiter in a competitive tech market” is specific and actionable. Good job identifying who experiences the problem.  
- Barrier/problem: Yes — “screening and prioritising candidates is slow and cognitively demanding” is a clear barrier. The root cause (“search tools rely mostly on titles and keywords”) is present but should be tied directly to the barrier sentence.  
- Outcome: You include measurable outcomes (double hiring‑manager interview pass‑through; reduce bias by 50%), but they are expressed alongside a proposed solution rather than as the user value you want to achieve.  
- Output vs outcome: The statement lists an output (a “candidate fit score agent” and its signals). Remember: an output is what you build (feature, agent); an outcome is the value for the user (faster shortlisting, higher interview conversion, less biased shortlists). Keep the problem statement focused on outcomes, not features.  
- Make outcomes tighter and measurable: add a baseline and timeframe (e.g. increase pass‑through from X% to Y% within Z months). Also define “reduce bias by 50%” — specify the metric (e.g. demographic parity in shortlist rates, reduction in false negatives for underrepresented groups).  
- Tighten phrasing and emotion: combine the cause with the barrier in one sentence, remove the feature list, and express the feeling succinctly (e.g. “frustrated” or “like I’m wasting time”). That keeps the problem statement empathetic and focused.  
- Suggested rewrite (template): “I am a senior in‑house recruiter in a competitive tech market trying to shortlist high‑fit candidates faster so hiring managers receive more interview‑ready people, but screening and prioritising is slow and cognitively demanding because search tools rely mostly on titles and keywords. This makes me feel frustrated and like I’m wasting time. Target outcome: double interview pass‑through rate and reduce shortlist bias by 50% within 6 months.”

I am trying to discover new trails to walk on.

- Outcome: Present but not measurable — “discover new trails” is a clear aim, but try to specify what “discover” means (identify, evaluate suitability, get route files) and a success criterion (e.g. find 3 suitable trails in one afternoon).

- Make the emotional/impact statement tighter: you already have “frustrated… when I try to plan long walks for my holidays” — that’s good; you can tie it to consequences (wasted planning time, risk of unsafe routes).
- Suggest a tightened example you can adapt: “I am a holiday hiker in Portugal trying to find and evaluate new long-distance trails, but I can’t easily access organised route data (maps, difficulty, GPX, logistics) because there’s no centralised source. - Keep it evidence-focused and outcome-oriented: prefer observable problems and measurable outcomes so you can validate the opportunity before jumping to solutions.

{"title": "Now it's your turn: Map out the big picture", "userText": "User Activities (Sticky Notes)
Access Clear, Simple Explanation of the Diagnosis
Find a Trusted Specialist and Next Steps
Share Experiences and Read Real Stories Anonymously
Monitor and Log Symptoms/Treatment Progress
Review Wellness and Lifestyle Guidance
Description of Main Activities
The activities users perform are derived from the features they prioritize and the information they seek to resolve their initial feelings of fear, confusion, and shame.
1. Access Clear, Simple Explanation of the Diagnosis
Goal: To overcome confusion ("I don't understand what's happening") and eliminate overwhelming, conflicting information.
Activity Details: The user will consume "Fragmentos educativos cortos sobre el VPH" (Educational short snippets about HPV) that offer "Explicaciones claras y simples (sin jerga médica)" (Clear, simple explanations without medical jargon). This activity fulfills the top priority of needing to "Entender mejor VPH (información clara)". The information must provide a detailed explanation of the diagnosis and steps to follow.
2. Find a Trusted Specialist and Next Steps
Goal: To gain certainty and trust in the medical path forward, addressing the anxiety around the future.
Activity Details: The user searches the Directory of HPV specialists/doctors and reviews resources providing Orientación sobre próximos pasos (Guidance on next steps). This is essential as users highly value "Professional medical guidance" and often require "Más detalle por la parte médica" or a clear plan.
3. Share Experiences and Read Real Stories Anonymously
Goal: To alleviate shame ("I feel broken / defective") and the feeling of being alone.
Activity Details: The user utilizes Anonymous community Q&A and peer support to connect with others. Trust is built by seeing "Historias reales de mujeres como yo" (real stories from women like me) and knowing "que otras mujeres se sienten igual" (that other women feel the same way). Many prefer this connection to be anonymous.
4. Monitor and Log Symptoms/Treatment Progress
Goal: To transition from fearful waiting ("I feel like I'm waiting for bad news") to informed, active control.
Activity Details: The user engages in Seguimiento de síntomas y recuperación (Symptom and recovery tracking). This feature supports the high priority of the "Ability to track my symptoms" (Rastrear mi salud / síntomas) and allows them to see patterns or data, which increases trust in the information.
5. Review Wellness and Lifestyle Guidance
Goal: To proactively support recovery and reduce general anxiety.
Activity Details: The user seeks out and implements Guía de nutrición y estilo de vida (Nutrition and lifestyle guidance) and Tips de bienestar emocional (Emotional wellness guides). This supports the overall goal of ensuring they are doing everything possible to manage their health.", "aiResponse": "Your user activities are well-defined and user-focused! Great job outlining the main activities! This comprehensive map will be invaluable in guiding your product development and ensuring you're addressing the core user needs.", "description": "What are the main activities your user will perform in your product to achieve their goals?

1. A specific target user

A good problem statement should follow the template: "I am [user] trying to [outcome], but [barrier] because [root cause]. This makes me feel [emotion]."

- Outcome: The value or benefit to the user (e.g., "helps me understand and control my business expenses")

Outcomes focus on the user's goals and the positive change they experience, while outputs are simply the tools or features that might help achieve those goals.

{"title": "Now it's your turn: Sketch all the moments inbetween", "userText": "The Journey: From Struggle to Climate-Positive Delivery
1. Overload and Constraint

The user is facing rapid urban growth, visible climate impacts, and pressure to deliver basic services. Projects are moving forward regardless, but climate ambition feels disconnected from day-to-day decisions. Carbon removal, adaptation finance, and co-benefits remain abstract and hard to operationalise.

How CAEBON helps:
CAEBON meets the user where they are — with imperfect data, limited time, and active projects already in motion.

2. First Signal: Seeing Opportunity Instead of Risk

The user inputs a location or project type into CAEBON. Instead of another risk map, they receive a clear signal: this place has climate-positive potential — across cooling, flood protection, materials, and carbon storage.

How CAEBON helps:
CAEBON reframes the problem from “what could go wrong” to “what could be unlocked here,” using local and global data to surface opportunity.

3. Translation into Actionable Pathways

CAEBON identifies a small number of feasible interventions — biochar soils, carbon-storing concrete, enhanced rock weathering, nature-based buffers, wastewater pathways — matched to the city’s context and constraints.

How CAEBON helps:
It translates global climate concepts into place-specific, infrastructure-relevant options that feel realistic rather than theoretical.

4. Co-benefits Made Visible

The user sees how the same project can deliver multiple outcomes: reduced heat, flood protection, improved air quality, benefits for vulnerable communities — not as separate initiatives, but as one integrated intervention.

How CAEBON helps:
By quantifying and narrating co-benefits, CAEBON gives the user language they can use with communities, leadership, and cross-departmental teams.

5. Finance Pathways Clarified

CAEBON links the project to potential funding routes: adaptation finance, blended finance, carbon markets, or public–private co-development — aligned with city plans and emerging standards.

How CAEBON helps:
It brings finance into the conversation early, reducing friction and increasing credibility before designs are locked in.

6. Confidence to Act and Advocate

Armed with a clear project brief, the user can advocate internally, engage partners, and approach funders with confidence. The project moves forward — not as a pilot, but as part of the city’s core development pathway.

How CAEBON helps:
CAEBON provides a defensible, shareable narrative that supports real decision-making.

7. Delivery and Legacy

The project breaks ground. It protects the city, stores carbon, improves daily life, and signals that this city can lead on climate-positive development — on its own terms.

{"title": "Now it's your turn: Describe your target user's struggle", "userText": "The Moment of Struggle

It’s late afternoon in a rapidly growing city.

Construction is everywhere — new housing, widened roads, informal settlements becoming permanent, drainage systems under strain. Heatwaves are lasting longer. Floods are becoming less predictable. Air quality complaints are rising. The population is growing faster than infrastructure capacity.

The user is at their desk in the city’s planning or resilience department.

They are juggling:

urgent service delivery issues,

a stack of project proposals that all claim to be “climate-aligned,”

emails from international funders asking for concepts, metrics, and alignment with global frameworks,

and internal pressure to act quickly, visibly, and credibly.

They know the city must build anyway — roads, housing, utilities, public spaces — whether or not perfect climate solutions are in place.

They also know that what gets built now will define the city for decades.

But right now, they are stuck.

What They Are Doing

They are trying to answer practical questions with limited tools:

Which of our planned projects could actually reduce heat, flooding, or pollution — not just meet minimum standards?

Where could we integrate carbon sinks, low-carbon materials, or nature-based solutions without increasing costs or complexity too much?

Which ideas are realistic enough to put in front of leadership, communities, or funders?

They toggle between:

spreadsheets,

PDFs from consultants,

global reports that feel abstract,

and their lived knowledge of the city.

In the evenings or early mornings, they are also studying — enrolled in an urban climate leadership course, attending webinars, reading reports — trying to translate global ideas into local action.

They care deeply about the communities they serve.
They are accountable to them.

What They Feel

They feel responsible, but underpowered.

They feel fatigue — not from lack of motivation, but from constantly trying to bridge the gap between ambition and capacity.

They feel pressure:

pressure to keep basic services running,

pressure to respond to climate impacts already happening,

pressure to position the city as “climate-ready” in a global landscape that rewards visibility and preparedness.

They are open to new solutions, but cautious.

They have seen:

pilots that never scale,

promises that don’t match reality,

tools built for cities with far more resources.

They are proud of their city and sceptical of anything that treats it as a case study rather than a partner.

What They Need in That Moment

They don’t need another report.

They don’t need another dashboard showing risk.

They need a signal.

A flare that shows:

This is where our city already has potential.

This is how our existing projects could become climate-positive.

This is why it matters for people — cooling, safety, health, jobs.

This is how it connects to real finance and real partners.

They need something they can:

understand quickly,

explain confidently,

and stand behind with integrity.

Name: Amina Okoye

About

Amina works in a fast-growing city in an emerging economy (e.g. Lagos, Accra, Nairobi, Dhaka, Medellín, Ho Chi Minh City). Her city is experiencing rapid population growth, infrastructure strain, heat stress, flooding, and air-quality challenges—often simultaneously.

She operates in a resource-constrained environment:

Small team, limited budget

Incomplete or outdated datasets

Heavy reliance on external consultants, donors, or development banks

Pressure to deliver tangible outcomes quickly

Her city is expanding now — new roads, housing, drainage, utilities — meaning decisions made today will lock in outcomes for decades.

She is increasingly aware of carbon removal, nature-based solutions, and climate-positive infrastructure, especially as international funders and standards (CRCF, Article 6, adaptation finance) enter the picture — but she lacks tools to operationalise them locally.

Frustrations

She is asked to plan for climate resilience with partial data.
Heat, flood, land use, and population data may exist — but are fragmented, inconsistent, or inaccessible.

Global climate tools don’t fit her reality.
Many platforms assume high-quality data, specialised teams, and long planning cycles that her city simply doesn’t have.

Carbon removal feels disconnected from urban decision-making.
CDR is discussed at the national or international level, but she has no way to assess how it applies to streets, housing, drainage, or public land in her city.

Funding conversations are opaque and late.
Projects are often designed first, then retrofitted to meet donor or finance requirements — leading to missed opportunities or rejection.

She is balancing urgency with uncertainty.
Infrastructure must be built now, even when perfect information isn’t available.

Goals

Make better decisions with imperfect data.
Amina wants tools that work even when data is incomplete, using global defaults and local overrides.

Identify climate-positive opportunities early.
She wants to know where infrastructure projects could deliver cooling, flood mitigation, social benefit, and carbon value — before designs are fixed.

Attract international finance without losing local relevance.
Success means projects that speak both:

the language of her city’s immediate needs, and

the language of global funders, carbon markets, and standards.

Reduce dependence on external consultants.
She wants internal capability — even lightweight — to screen and prioritise projects herself.

Build a healthier, more liveable city as it grows.
Her motivation is not compliance, but avoiding lock-in to unhealthy, high-carbon, inequitable urban form.

In one sentence

Amina needs a way to identify climate-positive, finance-ready infrastructure opportunities using limited data, so her rapidly growing city can build resilience and prosperity without waiting for perfect information or external consultants.

capital providers demanding clarity, comparability, and credibility.

{"text": "Feedback Plan
Who I will show it to

I will show the MVP to one urban climate practitioner working in a resource-constrained or rapidly urbanising city, ideally someone who:

Works in city planning, resilience, sustainability, or infrastructure delivery

Is familiar with climate risk, but not a carbon markets specialist

Has previously worked with donors, DFIs, or development banks

If needed, this could also be:

A practitioner at a development bank or climate fund who regularly reviews city project concepts.

How I will show it

A 30–45 minute live screen share (Zoom/Meet)

I will run the prototype; they will guide decisions and react in real time

No slide deck — only the product

What I will ask them to do

Describe a real project they are currently thinking about (street upgrade, housing, flood protection, etc.)

Use the MVP to:

select the project type

choose objectives

generate a project opportunity brief

Talk out loud while using it

What I will observe

Where they hesitate or ask clarifying questions

Whether they trust the signals or challenge assumptions

Which outputs they focus on first (methods, co-benefits, finance)

Whether the brief feels useful enough to share, or “interesting but not actionable”

What I will ask (5 core questions)

“Would this help you decide which projects to pursue or prioritise?”

“What feels missing or unclear for you to act on this?”

“What would make this credible enough to share with leadership or funders?”

“What part of this surprised you — positively or negatively?”

“If this existed tomorrow, how often would you use it?”

What success looks like

They say: “I would use this early in planning.”

They ask: “Can I export or share this?”

They suggest a second project to test — without prompting

That’s the signal you’re solving a real problem.

How I will capture learnings

Notes during the session (what confused them, what landed)

A short summary immediately after:

3 things to change

3 things to protect

1 feature to cut

Commitment

I will schedule at least one live feedback session within two weeks of the MVP being usable, and iterate the product based on what I observe — not what I assume.", "title": "Plan your first user test", "description": "Think of one way you'll get feedback: Who can you show your prototype or MVP to, and what will you ask them or observe?
